examples.title: SQL Transaction Example with COMMIT/ROLLBACK
examples.description: Demonstrates transaction handling in Python with SQLite database
examples.code:
import sqlite3

def transfer_funds(from_acc, to_acc, amount):
    conn = sqlite3.connect('bank.db')
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    
    try:
        # Start transaction implicitly
        cursor.execute("BEGIN")
        
        # Debit from source account
        cursor.execute("UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - ? WHERE id = ?", 
                      (amount, from_acc))
        
        # Credit to destination account
        cursor.execute("UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + ? WHERE id = ?", 
                      (amount, to_acc))
        
        # Check if debit was successful (balance >= 0)
        cursor.execute("SELECT balance FROM accounts WHERE id = ?", (from_acc,))
        balance = cursor.fetchone()[0]
        
        if balance < 0:
            raise ValueError("Insufficient funds")
        
        # Commit transaction
        cursor.execute("COMMIT")
        print("Transfer successful!")
        
    except Exception as e:
        # Rollback on any error
        cursor.execute("ROLLBACK")
        print(f"Transfer failed: {e}")
        
    finally:
        conn.close()

# Test the transfer
transfer_funds(1, 2, 500)
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates transaction handling with atomicity. The transfer either completes fully (both debit and credit) or rolls back completely if any error occurs, including insufficient funds. The COMMIT saves changes only if successful; ROLLBACK undoes everything on failure.

examples.title: Two-Phase Commit Protocol
examples.description: Pseudocode for distributed transaction commit protocol
examples.code:
PROCEDURE Two_Phase_Commit(coordinator, participants)
BEGIN
    // Phase 1: Prepare
    coordinator.sendPrepareToAll(participants)
    
    FOR each participant IN participants DO
        IF participant.canCommit THEN
            participant.sendVoteCommit()
            participant.lockResources()
            participant.writeToLog("READY")
        ELSE
            participant.sendVoteAbort()
            participant.writeToLog("ABORT")
        END IF
    END FOR
    
    // Phase 2: Decision
    IF coordinator.receivedAllVotesCommit() THEN
        coordinator.sendGlobalCommit()
        FOR each participant IN participants DO
            participant.commitTransaction()
            participant.releaseLocks()
            participant.writeToLog("COMMIT")
        END FOR
    ELSE
        coordinator.sendGlobalAbort()
        FOR each participant IN participants DO
            participant.rollbackTransaction()
            participant.releaseLocks()
            participant.writeToLog("ABORT")
        END FOR
    END IF
END
examples.explanation: The Two-Phase Commit ensures atomic commitment in distributed databases. Phase 1 (Prepare) asks all participants if they can commit - they lock resources and promise to commit. Phase 2 (Commit) only proceeds if all vote to commit; otherwise, all rollback. This ensures all-or-nothing behavior across distributed systems.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of participants - requires communication with all nodes
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) - stores state for each participant

examples.title: Lock-Based Concurrency Control (Pseudocode)
examples.description: Demonstrates basic locking mechanism to prevent lost updates
examples.explanation: This demonstrates exclusive locking (two-phase locking) to prevent concurrency problems. Transaction T1 acquires locks on both accounts before updating, ensuring no other transaction can read or modify until T1 commits. This prevents lost updates and ensures isolation. The lock() operation blocks other transactions until locks are released.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for lock/unlock operations, depends on lock manager
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of locked resources

examples.title: Transaction with Savepoints
examples.description: Demonstrates savepoint functionality for partial rollback in Python
examples.code:
import sqlite3

def process_order(order_id, items):
    conn = sqlite3.connect('shop.db')
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    
    try:
        cursor.execute("BEGIN")
        
        # Create order record
        cursor.execute("INSERT INTO orders (id, status) VALUES (?, 'processing')", 
                      (order_id,))
        
        # Set savepoint after order creation
        cursor.execute("SAVEPOINT order_created")
        
        for item in items:
            try:
                # Check stock
                cursor.execute("SELECT stock FROM products WHERE id = ?", 
                              (item['product_id'],))
                stock = cursor.fetchone()[0]
                
                if stock < item['quantity']:
                    # Rollback to savepoint, not entire transaction
                    cursor.execute("ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT order_created")
                    print(f"Insufficient stock for product {item['product_id']}")
                    continue
                
                # Update stock
                cursor.execute("UPDATE products SET stock = stock - ? WHERE id = ?",
                              (item['quantity'], item['product_id']))
                
                # Add order item
                cursor.execute("INSERT INTO order_items VALUES (?, ?, ?)",
                              (order_id, item['product_id'], item['quantity']))
                              
            except Exception as e:
                cursor.execute("ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT order_created")
                print(f"Error processing item: {e}")
        
        # Final commit
        cursor.execute("COMMIT")
        print(f"Order {order_id} processed successfully")
        
    except Exception as e:
        cursor.execute("ROLLBACK")
        print(f"Order processing failed: {e}")
        
    finally:
        conn.close()

# Example usage
order_items = [
    {'product_id': 101, 'quantity': 2},
    {'product_id': 102, 'quantity': 5},
    {'product_id': 103, 'quantity': 1}
]
process_order(5001, order_items)
examples.explanation: This demonstrates savepoints for granular transaction control. Instead of rolling back the entire transaction on error, we can rollback to a specific savepoint. This allows partial success - some items can be processed while others fail. The main transaction only commits if meaningful progress is made.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of items in the order
